raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.3,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "What level of improvement do you want? Choose one: (A) Light copyedit only (grammar, clarity, tone) — R1.6; (B) Copyedit plus restructuring and argument/claim alignment (thesis framing, logic, evidence-claim fit, section order) — R1.1, R1.2, R1.3, R1.5, R1.6; (C) All of B plus novelty positioning and literature citation/scholarship review — adds R1.4 and R1.7.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Sharpening the thesis and contribution framing above the sentence level is a common and central part of ‘improving’ a draft.",
    "R1.2": "The draft presents identification assumptions and conclusions that warrant checking for internal logical coherence.",
    "R1.3": "Claims about the ATE and subgroup effects should be checked for alignment with the reported analyses and results as written.",
    "R1.5": "Reordering and tightening sections (motivation, methods, data, identification, results, sensitivity, heterogeneity, conclusion) can materially improve clarity.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence- and paragraph-level polish is load-bearing for readability, concision, and professional tone.",
    "RX.1": "Edits must preserve and clearly communicate uncertainty (e.g., CIs, sensitivity bounds) and limitations.",
    "RX.2": "The improved draft should avoid overstating causal certainty given observational data and possible unobserved confounding.",
    "RX.3": "Work should stay within the scope of improving the provided text without introducing new analyses or unverifiable claims.",
    "RX.4": "Edits should not encourage overgeneralization that could mislead stakeholders or create downstream harms.",
    "RX.5": "Changes should be traceable to the source text with clear justifications to maintain provenance."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: What level of improvement do you want? Choose one: (A) Light copyedit only (grammar, clarity, tone) — R1.6; (B) Copyedit plus restructuring and argument/claim alignment (thesis framing, logic, evidence-claim fit, section order) — R1.1, R1.2, R1.3, R1.5, R1.6; (C) All of B plus novelty positioning and literature citation/scholarship review — adds R1.4 and R1.7.

rationale.R1.1: Sharpening the thesis and contribution framing above the sentence level is a common and central part of ‘improving’ a draft.
rationale.R1.2: The draft presents identification assumptions and conclusions that warrant checking for internal logical coherence.
rationale.R1.3: Claims about the ATE and subgroup effects should be checked for alignment with the reported analyses and results as written.
rationale.R1.5: Reordering and tightening sections (motivation, methods, data, identification, results, sensitivity, heterogeneity, conclusion) can materially improve clarity.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level polish is load-bearing for readability, concision, and professional tone.
rationale.RX.1: Edits must preserve and clearly communicate uncertainty (e.g., CIs, sensitivity bounds) and limitations.
rationale.RX.2: The improved draft should avoid overstating causal certainty given observational data and possible unobserved confounding.
rationale.RX.3: Work should stay within the scope of improving the provided text without introducing new analyses or unverifiable claims.
rationale.RX.4: Edits should not encourage overgeneralization that could mislead stakeholders or create downstream harms.
rationale.RX.5: Changes should be traceable to the source text with clear justifications to maintain provenance.
